title: Tuples
subtitle: Immutable sequences for fixed collections of data
learning_objectives: Create tuples using parentheses and understand their immutability
Explain why immutability makes tuples safer for constants
Pack multiple values into a tuple
Unpack a tuple into individual variables
Create a single-element tuple with the required trailing comma

sections.heading: What Is a Tuple and Why Use It?
sections.content:
A tuple is an ordered, immutable sequence enclosed in parentheses (). Immutable means you cannot add, remove, or change elements after creation — Python will raise a TypeError if you try. This makes tuples ideal for data that should never change: coordinates, RGB colors, database records, or function return values. Tuples are also slightly faster than lists and can be used as dictionary keys (lists cannot).

To create a single-element tuple, you must include a trailing comma: (42,). Without the comma, Python treats the parentheses as grouping, not a tuple.
sections.code_example:
# Creating tuples
coords = (10, 20)
rgb = (255, 128, 0)
one = (42,)          # single-element — comma required!

print(coords)        # (10, 20)
print(type(one))     # <class 'tuple'>
print(type((42)))    # <class 'int'>  — no comma, not a tuple!

# Tuples support indexing and len like lists
print(coords[0])     # 10
print(len(rgb))      # 3

sections.heading: Packing and Unpacking
sections.content: Tuple packing assigns multiple values to one tuple variable. Tuple unpacking does the reverse: it assigns each element of a tuple to a separate variable in a single statement. The number of variables on the left must match the number of elements in the tuple. Unpacking makes code readable and is widely used — for example, with enumerate() and dict.items().
sections.code_example:
# Packing
point = (3, 7)

# Unpacking
x, y = point
print(x)   # 3
print(y)   # 7

# Swap two variables with tuple unpacking
a = 10
b = 20
a, b = b, a
print(a, b)   # 20 10

# Unpacking a 3-element tuple
name, age, city = ("Alice", 30, "Paris")
print(name, age, city)

exercises.title: Create and Inspect a Tuple
exercises.prompt: Create a tuple called dimensions with three values: 1920, 1080, and 32. Print the tuple, its length, and the first element.
exercises.starter_code:
# Create a tuple called dimensions with the values 1920, 1080, and 32

# Print the tuple

hints: len() and indexing work exactly the same as with lists

tests.message: Your code should run without any errors
The length of the tuple should be 3

exercises.title: Unpack a Tuple
exercises.prompt: Given the tuple person = ("Bob", 25, "Engineer"), unpack it into three variables: name, age, and job. Then print each variable on its own line.
exercises.starter_code:
person = ("Bob", 25, "Engineer")

# Unpack the tuple

# Print each variable
exercises.solution:
person = ("Bob", 25, "Engineer")
name, age, job = person
print(name)
print(age)
print(job)
hints: Unpacking syntax: name, age, job = person
There must be exactly three variables on the left to match the three tuple elements

tests.message: Your code should run without any errors

quiz.question: What is the type of (99) in Python?
quiz.explanation: Parentheses without a trailing comma are just grouping operators. (99) is simply the integer 99. To create a single-element tuple you need (99,) with the trailing comma.

quiz.question: Which statement about tuples is true?
options: Tuples can be modified after creation
Tuples are immutable — elements cannot be changed after creation
quiz.explanation: Tuples are immutable sequences. Once created, you cannot add, remove, or reassign elements. This is the key difference from lists.

completion_message: Solid understanding of tuples! Knowing when to use an immutable tuple versus a mutable list is a mark of good Python style. Next up: dictionaries.
